title: Honoring the legacy of the Harlem Renaissance, a cultural, social, and political movement in American history.
plain_summary: This bill honors the Harlem Renaissance, which was an important time in the 1910s to 1930s when African Americans in Harlem, New York created amazing art, music, and books. During this time, Black people gained pride and were able to control how they were seen in American culture. The Harlem Renaissance also helped start the civil rights movement that came later. The bill supports keeping Harlem's history safe and protecting the neighborhood from changes that might hurt its cultural importance.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution honors the legacy of the Harlem Renaissance, which was a cultural, social, and political movement in American history.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: The Harlem Renaissance completely changed how Black people were seen and understood in America. It still influences artists, writers, and thinkers today, creating lasting effects on culture in the United States and around the world. Between the nineteen tens and nineteen seventies, about six million Black people moved from the American South to Northern, Midwestern, and Western states. This huge movement of people is called the Great Migration. It was one of the largest movements of people in United States history. People were trying to escape the extremely racist Jim Crow segregation laws. During the Great Migration, Black people from Southern states and people from Africa living in the Caribbean and Latin America came north looking for better jobs, housing, education, and civil rights. Even though they faced segregation and racial discrimination in the Army, they served as patriotic Americans during World War One. The most famous African American military unit in World War One was the three hundred sixty ninth Infantry Regiment, known as the Harlem Hellfighters or Black Rattlers. Most were New Yorkers, including Black and Latino residents of Harlem. They entered battle on October fifteenth, nineteen eighteen, and fought bravely in the Meuse-Argonne Offensive alongside French troops. The French Government gave them their highest honor, the Croix de Guerre. Under Lieutenant James Reese Europe's leadership, the three hundred sixty ninth Regiment's military band became famous for ragtime music and is credited with bringing American jazz to Europe. When they returned to the United States, thousands of people came to their parade on February seventeenth, nineteen nineteen. This parade is considered the start of the Harlem Renaissance era. Harlem in New York City became one of the most popular places for these families to move. It had been an all White neighborhood, but by the nineteen twenties it became home to about two hundred thousand to five hundred thousand African Americans. This large population shift led to the birth of an African American cultural movement. Important organizations like the National Urban League and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People provided support. Well known leaders like W.E.B. Du Bois, James Weldon Johnson, Walter White, and Jessie Fauset gave artistic guidance and money to support this cultural awakening that lasted from the nineteen tens to the mid nineteen thirties. They helped people overcome segregated churches, schools, housing, stores, theaters, hotels, and restaurants that continued in some form in Harlem until the nineteen sixties. In nineteen fourteen, Marcus Garvey from Jamaica founded the United Negro Improvement Association. He wanted stronger connections between African Americans and African people around the world. He was also an important thinker during the Harlem Renaissance. Educator, writer, and philosopher Alain Locke put together a book collection called the New Negro that showed the work of African Americans seeking social, political, and artistic change. The name New Negro came to define the movement we now call the Harlem Renaissance. Statesman Asa Philip Randolph helped start our nation's first federally recognized labor union to protect Black workers. He founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters in nineteen twenty five in Harlem. Bold and exciting forms of Black music developed, along with places to listen and dance like Harlem's Minton's Playhouse, the Cotton Club, Small's Paradise, Baby Grand, Lenox Lounge, Savoy Ballroom, Renaissance Ballroom, and Alhambra Ballroom. Blues, ragtime, and jazz became the main forms of cultural expression in Harlem. Legendary artists like Scott Joplin, Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington, Cab Calloway, Lena Horne, Bessie Smith, Count Basie, Ethel Waters, Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, and many other performers, composers, and arrangers became famous. Edward Kennedy Duke Ellington worked with Billy Strayhorn, who was gay and affectionately called Sweet Pea. Ellington was a composer, pianist, and jazz orchestra leader for five decades who wrote over one thousand songs. Louis Armstrong, known as the Jazz Ambassador, became one of the most influential musicians in jazz history with his distinctive voice and unique style. This era also saw the growth of literary work by and about African Americans. Writers like Sterling A. Brown, Alice Dunbar Nelson, Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Nella Larson, Wallace Thurman, and Countee Cullen created important works. Two of the first works that significantly impacted Harlem Renaissance culture were poetry collections called Harlem Shadows by Claude McKay and Cane by Jean Toomer. Artist Aaron Douglas defined a modern visual language that showed Black Americans in a new light and later became known as the father of African American art. Other important artists included sculptor Richard Barthe and leading women artists Augusta Savage and Elizabeth Catlett. Famous Black architects including Vertner Woodson Tandy, George Washington Foster, John Lewis Wilson, and Norma Merrick Sklarek helped transform the Harlem landscape. Acclaimed photographers James Van Der Zee, Morgan and Marvin Smith, Austen Hansen, and Gordon Parks were known as the unofficial chroniclers of African American life in Harlem. Their many formal and informal photographs provide a lasting visual record of this moment in history. The Harlem Renaissance is remembered for important literature including Home to Harlem by Claude McKay, Not Without Laughter by Langston Hughes, The Infants of the Spring by Wallace Thurman, Smoke Lillies and Jade by Richard Bruce Nugent, Black No More by George Schuyler, Their Eyes Were Watching God by Zora Neale Hurston, and The Living is Easy by publisher and writer Dorothy West. The development of Harlem as a Black cultural center in the early twentieth century created social and artistic movements that produced a golden era in African American culture. This showed up in dance, design, architecture, poetry, literature, politics, fashion, scholarship, music, stage performance, and art. Harlem attracted an amazing concentration of smart and talented people and served as the symbolic capital of this cultural awakening. Other New York City neighborhoods and communities in Chicago, Cleveland, and Los Angeles also supported social and creative movements. The Harlem Renaissance included literary, musical, theatrical, and visual arts. Its participants also tried to create a new image of the Negro that challenged racist and insulting stereotypes that White people had used to characterize Black people. Instead, they wanted to create pride in Black culture and heritage. Important to the movement were magazines like The Crisis published by the NAACP, Opportunity published by the National Urban League, and The Messenger, a socialist journal eventually connected with the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters. The Harlem Renaissance was not controlled by one particular way of thinking but was characterized by intense debate. The movement laid a foundation for later African American literature and consciousness worldwide.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives Resolves
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes that the Harlem Renaissance was a crucial moment in American history for African Americans and the African diaspora community of intellectuals, artists, performers, writers, poets, and musicians. Their works of political discussion and creative expression created a path for self determination and self empowerment that is still evident today. The House recognizes that the Harlem Renaissance gave Black people pride in and control over how the Black experience was shown in American culture. It set the stage for the civil rights movement and continues to inform today's activities for social justice and equal rights. The House supports efforts to recognize, promote, preserve, and celebrate the rich history of the Harlem Renaissance and its continuation through modern interpretive programs. The House protects Harlem's historic assets, its cultural legacy, and the rich history of its people against threats that result in the displacement and uprooting of low and moderate income residents. These threats undermine its world renowned cultural identity and unique neighborhood character, which are economic tools not only for Harlem but for New York City in general. The House celebrates the lasting impact of the Harlem Renaissance on the art, literature, music, discussion, and culture of the United States.
